label: Thammasat (dhammasattha)

scripts.label: Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา

materials.label: Mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา

samples: Kadi lok kadi tham manglai thammasat

lede: Thammasat (dhammasattha) accounts for 20 catalogued manuscripts. It clusters in Lampang (25% of the corpus for this subject), ahead of Chiang Mai and Phrae. Nearly all (100%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to palm-leaf · ใบลาน (90%) over mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (5%). Dated witnesses run 1817–1947 CE (10 of 20 carry a date).

authored.title: Thammasat (dhammasattha)
authored.body_html:
What this is

The thammasat (ธรรมศาสตร์; Pali dhammasattha) is the cosmic law-treatise — the frame that tells a Southeast Asian legal world where law comes from and why it binds. In the tradition's own telling, the dhammasattha is not made by kings but discovered: a primordial sage reads the eternal law written at the boundary-wall of the universe and brings it back for humankind. It is the _kadi tham_ pole of law — the dhamma-law — that stands above and legitimates the king's positive code, the Mangraisat.

What the catalogue holds

Twenty witnesses carry a thammasat / dhammasat name, classed as customary law. Set beside the twenty-four Mangraisat witnesses, the catalogue holds both poles of the Thai legal cosmos in near-equal number — a genuinely valuable pairing.

Inference — that the two clusters are of comparable size is itself a finding: this collection preserves the whole legal architecture, not just its enforceable half. The thammasat supplies the theory (law as discovered cosmic order); the Mangraisat supplies the practice (a named king's enforceable code). Having both in one corpus is what lets the archive show law as the tradition itself understood it — a single structure with a heavenly frame and an earthly body.

Beyond the catalogue — law read off the wall of the world

Tradition — the following is background from the wider Thai–Burmese–Mon legal tradition, not established from these specific manuscripts; treat as orienting, not authoritative.

  • The discovered, not decreed, law. The dhammasattha's defining claim is that law is eternal and found, not legislated — the sage Manu (or a comparable primordial seer) perceives it at the edge of the cosmos and transmits it. This is what gives the earthly code its authority: a king enforces the law, he does not invent it.
  • A shared regional inheritance. The dhammasattha tradition runs across the Mon, Burmese and Tai worlds; the Lanna thammasat is the northern Thai branch of a trans-regional legal literature, which is part of why it recurs in so many copies.
  • The paired frame in practice. kadi tham (the cosmic-law cases the thammasat governs) and kadi lok (the worldly cases the Mangraisat governs) are the two halves a premodern Thai judge worked between — the reason these two entities belong read together.

Notes

  • A well-attested anchor, best read as a pair. The hard fact is n = 20, and its near-parity with the Mangraisat's n = 24 is the real prize. The cosmological framing above is clearly-marked tradition; the two clusters are hard data.
  • Next move — the two-pole comparison. Inference — the highest-value pass here is to read the thammasat and Mangraisat witnesses against each other (via `raw_metadata` and page-content), surfacing how the discovered-law frame and the king's-law code cite and depend on one another within this single collection.
